narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and middle-family income background, you've put in the effort to pursue law school, including planning to attend full-time like most students. The admissions model we use predicted a 47% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is unfortunately high enough to tip the scales toward rejecting your application—it's designed to flag risks based on patterns from thousands of past students to help schools admit those most likely to succeed. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score of 24, which stands out well below the typical 36.7 average and strongly signaled higher risk to the model. On a brighter note, your Year 3 school decile of 7—above the average of 5.53—had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward bar passage and showing your later academic strength. Your Year 1 decile of 7, also above average, helped in a similar way, along with your undergrad GPA right around the 3.21 norm, both nudging things toward success. Even your middle-income bracket and full-time status provided small boosts compared to the broader student pool. Overall, while your grades and background compare favorably to typical applicants, that lower LSAT score carried the most weight in this close call, outweighing your other strengths. It's tough to hear, especially when you've shown real progress in school deciles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score—many students see big gains with focused prep—and reapply with that update, as it could flip the model's view dramatically. Your full-time commitment and solid GPA already position you well above average in key areas. We're rooting for you; persistence pays off for determined applicants like yourself. Reach out to admissions for personalized advice on strengthening your profile. You've got this potential—keep pushing.
